turns.text: If Your Honor pleases. If I may say so, the labor law in Florida developed and was elucidated through litigation. This had not -- this Court had not faced this type of assault --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean this -- this rehearing.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: The Supreme Court --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not talking about those.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- Supreme Court of Florida. Supreme Court of Florida.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) Florida law.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I -- that's what I'm talking. The Supreme Court of Florida is what I'm talking about. The Supreme Court of Florida had not faced a mass of labor litigation under its labor statute until this hearing.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, was the statute? Would you state that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes. The statute, I shall --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What was these things that bounded by statute so far as Florida legislation?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: The statute, if Your Honor pleases, is found on page 2 of our brief, the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- full statute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- state the requirement of that with --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: The --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- so far as relevant to this case.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: The statute gives to the employee the right to self-organization and to bargain through a bargaining agent of its own or their own choice. That's what the statute affords them without -- and makes it unlawful for anyone to intimidate or coerce employee to deny him that right. That's what law of Florida was at the time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Without particularization, without details.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Without particularization, without details.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what was the decision of law?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: May I -- before you get to the decision, may I just ask you if those -- anything in the law which required the employees to have a majority of this -- of them and if they did -- didn't have a majority, would they still organize and represent those who did belong to the union?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That question has not, Your Honor, been specifically answered --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- as I understand it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: We have taken the position, and I think correctly so that collective bargaining means a bargaining agent for a unit and it requires a majority. But it is not in the statute and I cannot --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, let me ask you this. I -- I assume that they did have a majority, could they bargain, under this Florida law, for all of the employees of the hotel or could they only bargain for themselves?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: They could only bargain for themselves, Your Honor, because they have the right to a statute and that -- and that I think keeps the avenue of employment open both to union and non-union.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Now -- now I interrupted your answer to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what I want to know is --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- if the statute is silent on the question of a majority.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So your Supreme Court silent on that subject. From my point of view, the Supreme Court of Florida has been entitled to lay down rules if it is a legislature of Florida. What I want to know is did the Supreme Court of Florida, before this litigation arose, lay down any rule on that matter?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It is laid down a rule in this case?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I so understood the Fontainebleau case to mean, and that is my interpretation of it, to me --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All I got from your elucidation up to now was, that's quite erroneously, that the Florida Supreme Court indicated and laid down guides for the lower courts of Florida for the Chancellors that there must be a majority in order to put the picketing, is that right or wrong?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: No. It did not, Your Honor, in the first place.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, what was it that it told -- why didn't it tell the lower courts to issue injunctions? On what basis?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: On the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Violence?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: On -- on the basis -- the original position, Your Honor, was on the basis that the union did not represent the employees that had not demonstrated to the employees that represented the employees that had suddenly without any fair place for picket lines.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: By represent five out of 500 or a majority out of 500? I'm just asking for something that ought to be answerable from what was written in the opinion of the Supreme Court. I'm assuming oral argument is intended to enlighten the Court and does not require me to read your brief in order to understand what your argument is. I don't mean to say I don't need them read eventually but I do not want to read briefs, most of the time, before argument.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I'm entitled to have the case made clear by oral arguments.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, if Your Honor pleases, the Supreme Court of Florida had not laid down a rule in reference to other -- in order to require the majority at the time that picketing commenced.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, what did it lay down? What kind of a rule did it lay down to guide the Chancellors?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: It laid down a rule that this -- this union had moved in representing no one.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Nobody.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That was -- that was the proof at the moment that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, does the record -- did the -- are the records in the cases that are before us bare out a justification of the injunction on that basis --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Your Honor --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- namely, that this union represented no employee.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Your -- if Your Honor pleases --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I can understand the public policy. I don't mean I don't understand it. I both understand it. Now, I think it's constitutionally invalid for Florida to have such a policy.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I want to know what it is --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- before I can tell whether it's valid.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: If Your Honor pleases, at the Fontainebleau case, if Your Honor please, the Court laid its opinion for the injunction on two things. One, there is violence and two, that the union did not represent the -- a majority of the employees or a substantial number. That's what I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) found those two facts with reference to Fontainebleau doesn't give any basis for me to find that those facts existed with reference to two other cases and it so happens to other cases that are along before this Court. So you have to do this, if I may say so. You have to establish that this record comes within the determinations of Fontainebleau even on violence or on a representation to (Inaudible) Can you do that?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I think I can do that, if Your Honor pleases, if I will have the time.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if you don't do that, where are you?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if you don't do that, where are you?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, I'm here, if Your Honor pleases. I'm in the situation where this -- the States, this State has determined that this union moved in for the purpose of coercing employees to designate it as their bargaining agent where the policy of the State says they have no power to do that by picketing.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And does your record sustain your claim that there was coercion in any sense that that term has legal meaning?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well [Laughs] that's difficult to know what that term -- what legal meaning that term has. But as I understand it, where you're driven off and where you're afraid to -- to cross a picket line and where your employees have to live upstairs in your luxury hotel in order to wait on the customers, that has some evidence of coercion. Now, my time is -- I want to have a moment or two --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I would just like to ask one more question --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- and that is whether there is any finding by any court in any of these cases that are before us that the union put a picket line without any -- representing any employees in that institution.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, all I can say, if Your Honor pleases, is that the record before the Supreme Court of Florida saw or established the fact by the designation cards put in evidence by the union itself which have not been brought here and which the Supreme Court of Florida points to as the basis for its conclusion.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, did they -- did the Supreme Court, in words, find that at the time these people put up a picket line on any of these institutions that it did not have any members?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: No, sir. It did not make such a finding.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, then -- then what are we arguing about here when you -- when you say that these people put up a picket line without any -- representing any employees? Where do we base it upon if we don't base it upon -- where do you base this on upon --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I base --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- if this isn't --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- finding of any court?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I base it on this. We are talking about different injunctions at different times. Did -- I never --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I -- I asked you about anyone --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- just give me anyone.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: At the time that the Supreme Court ordered the injunctive orders originally, the sworn complaint and the evidence taken upon which an injunctive order maybe granted established that the union represented no one. They put in no evidence in at that time --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- and put no answer in it at that time.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- but you had a hearing after that and the Chancellor filed that those things were true.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, all the Chancellors -- the Chancellor found that they represented less than a majority and the Supreme --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- Court found they represented insignificant number.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But less than a majority is more than nothing, isn't it? And you have been talking about nothing. You have been -- I feel -- feel that [Laughs] -- that the way as I read you.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, I -- I'm afraid Your Honor's statement is --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I base --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- absolutely correct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I -- I mean -- I mean you've been saying if they had no -- they had no -- no employees at all that they represented. Now, I'm trying to find out where -- what you rely on to represent to us that at the time they put up these picket lines, they represented no one.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, I can only call Your Honors' attention in view of the fact the evidence is not here. And it was not our job to -- to demonstrate to the Supreme Court of Florida's findings of fact are not supported.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, if the evidence isn't here, I must interrupt you, if the evidence isn't here, speaking for myself, this Court is incompetent to set aside the judgment below, but is it true that the evidence isn't here?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, I think we demonstrate that in our brief. If Your Honor will turn to page 17 when we quote from our brief in the Supreme Court of Florida, we point out a number of places this union claimed to represented or had designation cards on at the time the picket line grew up. And that was based on the record that has not been brought here. But that's a statement they put in the record here and I referred to. That's all I can refer to.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But did you say there that they represented no one at the time these pickets were put out?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Read it to us --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- then.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Those cards indicated the Algiers Hotel, who has not here, one employee had designated the union as its bargaining agent prior to the picket line. In the Sans Souci Hotel case, two employees that this case that had 300 employees, two employees that visited the union prior to the picket line. The Monte Carlo case, not here, none of the employees had designated the union prior to the picket line. And the Sorrento Hotel case, not here, three of the employees had designated the union prior to the picket line and the Sherry Frontenac Hotel, which is here, one employee had designated the union prior to the picket line. And the Roney Plaza Hotel case, no employee had designated the union prior to the picket line.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, that is your statement. What I would like to know is was there any finding to that effect by any court?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Only the finding that I referred to that the Supreme Court of Florida said that it -- an insignificant number.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was that after the proceedings to vacate the injunction?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's after the injunction was vacated.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That was after the injunction was vacated.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what I want to know is this, and I can assure you that I haven't the slightest notion what I think about this case at this point because my mind isn't (Inaudible) based on Goldberg painted one nice picture and you painted another nice picture. But it ought to be susceptible of some specific actions to what seems to be relevant questions, namely, has your Supreme Court laid down, since the statute hasn't, that picketing presupposes, the right of picketing presupposes representation by the union of a majority of the employees. And you said no to that, isn't it not?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The next question is has your Supreme Court said, as a matter of law, picketing can be valid, must represent -- must be represented by a union that has a substantial, whatever that may mean?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: It has so said.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now -- and therefore, the question is --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's so said.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- whether a State can lay down the requirements of substantiality on the part of the strike and picketing.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: It has so said in --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It has so said.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you say that's the governing Florida principle and the question is whether that's valid or invalid under the Fourteenth Amendment, is that right? Is that your position?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what I want to know is, next, out in the -- these two fat volumes, I should think these -- these volumes are so fat, would have all the relevant facts in them, do these -- do these records contain the basis for determining whether, as a matter of fact, the Supreme Court was justified in saying it was not a substantial representation?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: It does not, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is there were -- there were -- there was evidence, there were affidavits, there were legal materials in these proceedings which have not been brought up before us, is that right?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's correct. That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In these two cases?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't understand that.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: All right, sir. I didn't think, if Your Honor pleases, that it was our --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It wasn't your business.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: All right, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Certainly not.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Your Honor has given --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Was unlimited --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- five minutes additional, may I take it at this time? I have my lights up and my time is up, and I -- I don't know what the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, no, no. You have -- you have much time left to this.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, I have an associate --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, oh, well, your division of time. You may --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: All right, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- you may make as you wish.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: All right, sir. I -- I would like to touch the legal phase of the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, you may.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: -- problem, if I may.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You may.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Now, I think that the Supreme Court of the United States in the (Inaudible) case has determined the first problem here involved of whether or not a court may enjoin picketing when it's for an unlawful purpose even though the picketing is assumed to be peaceful. I'm not going to spend any time on that. Counsel recognized, I think it does govern the case. But they expressed the hope in prayer that in some case in which there's been a certiorari granted, there will be a -- a retreat from that position or a change of that position. I must take a moment to get to the Guss and the Fairlawn and the San Diego cases in which it is contended now that the National Labor Relations Act preempted the trial court from rendering an -- an injunction and preempted the field or took from the trial court its jurisdiction to act. Those cases, I think, are involved here. And those cases present, really, the only substantial question as I understand it in the -- as I see it in the record. Now, this presents an unusual problem because the trial court that heard this case, the Circuit Court, is a court of general chancery jurisdiction. And it has the ancient jurisdiction, the traditional jurisdiction to enjoin anyone, union or otherwise that interfere unlawfully with another's business and -- and causes irreparable injury. The problem here presented is when and how that traditional and historic chancery jurisdiction of a court of general jurisdiction is preempted and ousted. In the Guss case, there was an admission that the -- the employer was engaged in interstate commerce. And I think, in that case, the Utah Board was actually enforcing rights under the Labor Act, National Labor Act.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Are these hotels in commerce? So do you contend these are not (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: They're not inference.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: They're not.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: They are not, Your Honor. Now, that presents the first problem.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: As I the read the record -- would you mind turning to record page 53?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: In that first paragraph, an affidavit by a man with the name of Smith has been tendered on these issues of commerce.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Page 53, Your Honor?

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: 53 of the record.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: 53. That's the final decree.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: The final decree on (Inaudible)

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's the final decree.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Yes, final decree. The recital in that is that that affidavit --

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, I -- I --

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- was -- was objected to on the ground that it was immaterial and irrelevant.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Yes, that's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: And the Court sustained the objection upon that ground.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Now, why is it immaterial and doesn't that present a question of law for us under the Guss case?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, that is precisely the problem I was moving to when the (Inaudible) Now, the problem here is when and how is the traditional chancery jurisdiction of this trial court ousted? When must the question be raised? How must it be raised? May it be waived? Now, that's a field in which there has been nothing said as far as I can find as to the method and manner of raising the problem.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: That's a matter of state procedure, isn't it?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: That would be a matter of state procedure.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: If it's a matter of state procedure, then, Your Honor, made in court.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: But the -- the -- in the -- in final injunction, the Court passes on that very issue here.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: If Your Honor will permit me, I will get to that. Just give me (Inaudible) please. I don't mean to be (Inaudible) but my time is (Inaudible) Now, the union in each case filed an answer not raising this question but seeking affirmative relief under the right to wake statute of our State and invoked the jurisdiction of the Court. That answer was filed on May 9th in most of these cases. The complaints were filed in April. They were winning. And this union has a record, and we pointed to it, of resisting it all the time that the National Labor Relations Board ever had jurisdiction of the hotel industry. They have taken that position before the Board. And we cite the case where this particular union took that position. Then, they did not take the position in the state court. As long as they were winning, as long as the injunctions were being denied, and they were winning and continuing to win, remember, if Your Honors please, we took all the appeals but the lesser. We took the place 6, 8, 10. They were happy with the jurisdiction. They saw nothing in it to justify any contention that the National Labor Relations Board had anything to do with this, nothing in the pleadings for the first 60 days of this litigation ever suggested that we were in commerce or that the National Labor Relations Board had any jurisdiction. In the cases before Judge Floyd, Judge Crawford, Judge Gordon, they never even filed this answer, this supplemental answer which was filed out of turn raising this question until after the cases were on appeal at the final hearing. We stipulated that they had misfiled them. And they could assume that they were filed as of June 28th.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That -- and that would be timely, would it?

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: Oh, no, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, no, your stipulation.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: No, sir, it would not.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: It -- it would not make them timely.

turns.name: MARION E. SIBLEY
turns.text: No, it would not make them timely. They have no right to file without leave of court for 20 days after the case becomes an issue. I say true, this was much after that and was filed without leave of court. Now, in the Fairlawn case, the -- the Chief Justice has clearly pointed out, has clearly pointed out that throughout that litigation in the state court, there was always the question raised and urged in the National Labor Relations Board and the National -- preempted the Court. Justice pointed out. And it was not pointed out, I assume. And I -- it was pointed out because this Court in its inception had jurisdiction of this litigation at this class of cases, but something must be done and must be done timely. And in other cases, this Court had said there had been no suggestion when this question was raised that is frivolous or that is dilatory. Now, I ask, I ask, a court of general jurisdiction who perceives in a case -- in a class of cases that has jurisdiction made the union come in and litigate like the benefit of its decrees, like we find itself until the last moment and then filed in every case what they call the supplemental answer, and then we all stipulate the final decree so we'll get the cases on with and then he says I don't want to introduce an affidavit. And we will stipulate that we'll go in and get final decrees, final decree, you read on the record. We've got an affidavit for a man named Smith. And stipulate here that you -- that you objected on the ground that it's immaterial and irrelevant. And the final decrees are drawn. And the trial court, not a single trial court, except one, ever knew that this issue was involved in this litigation.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now.
